12 Jul 2025

The Weight of Sequential Monte Carlo

Weight Watchers

$$ \begin{align*} \mathbb{E}_p[X] &= \int x \ p(x) dx \\ &= \int \frac{q(x)}{q(x)} \ x \ p(x) dx \\ &= \int \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} \ x \ q(x) dx \\ &= \mathbb{E}_q \left[\frac{p(x)}{q(x)} \ x \right] \\ &= \mathbb{E}_q \left[w(x) \ x \right] \end{align*} $$

Normalize weights

\[\begin{align*} \tilde{w}_i &= \frac{w(x_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^N w(x_j)} \\ &= \frac{w(x_i)}{\frac{N}{N}\sum_{j=1}^N w(x_j)} \\ &= \frac{w(x_i)}{N\sum_{j=1}^N \frac{1}{N} w(x_j)} \\ &= \frac{w(x_i)}{N \mathbb{E}_q[ w(x)]} \\ \end{align*}\]

Technically,

\[\begin{align*} \tilde{w}_i &= \frac{w(x_i)}{N \mathbb{E}_q[ w(x)]} \\ &= \frac{w(x_i)}{N \int q(x) \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} dx} \\ &= \frac{w(x_i)}{N \int p(x) dx} \\ &= \frac{w(x_i)}{N} \end{align*}\]

Effective Sample Size (ESS)

1. The Necessity

Suppose you want to estimate $\mathbb{E}_p[f(X)]$ for some target distribution $p$. In a perfect world, you’d draw $N$ i.i.d. samples from $p$ and compute the sample mean. The variance of that estimator drops as $1/N$ — clean and predictable.

But in practice, your samples are almost never i.i.d. from the target:

  • MCMC produces samples that are serially correlated (each sample depends on the previous one).
  • Importance sampling draws from a proposal $q \neq p$, then corrects with weights — but those weights can be wildly uneven.
  • Particle filters resample with replacement, creating duplicate particles.

In all these cases, your $N$ raw samples carry less information than $N$ truly independent samples would. You need a single number that answers: “how many i.i.d. samples is my collection of correlated/weighted samples actually worth?” That number is the ESS.

Without it, you’re flying blind — you can’t meaningfully compare algorithms, diagnose poor mixing, set stopping criteria, or calibrate your confidence in Monte Carlo estimates.

2. The Intuition

Think of it this way. You have $N$ people in a room voting on something, but some people’s votes count more than others (importance weights), or groups of people always vote together (correlation).

  • If one person holds 99% of the voting power, you effectively have 1 voter.
  • If everyone votes identically (perfect correlation), you effectively have 1 voter.
  • If everyone has equal weight and votes independently, you have $N$ effective voters.

ESS quantifies the “democratic health” of your sample. It measures how much of your nominal sample size $N$ is actually doing useful work.

The core principle: match the variance. If your fancy estimator has the same variance as a simple average of $N_{\text{eff}}$ i.i.d. samples, then your effective sample size is $N_{\text{eff}}$.

3. Setup and Derivation

3.1 The Importance Sampling Case

This is the most commonly cited derivation. We want to compute:

\[\mu = \mathbb{E}_p[f(X)] = \int f(x)\, p(x)\, dx\]

We can’t sample from $p$ directly, so we draw $X_1, \dots, X_N \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} q$ from a proposal distribution $q$, and use the importance sampling identity:

\[\mu = \int f(x) \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} q(x)\, dx = \mathbb{E}_q\!\left[f(X)\,w(X)\right]\]

where $w(x) = p(x)/q(x)$ are the (unnormalized) importance weights.

Deriving the self-normalized estimator

The problem with standard importance sampling

If we know $p$ exactly (including its normalizing constant), life is simple. The standard IS estimator is:

\[\hat{\mu}_{\text{IS}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w(X_i)\, f(X_i), \qquad w(x) = \frac{p(x)}{q(x)}\]

This is unbiased: $\mathbb{E}q[\hat{\mu}{\text{IS}}] = \mathbb{E}_q[w(X)f(X)] = \int f(x)\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}q(x)\,dx = \int f(x)\,p(x)\,dx = \mu$. Done.

But in practice — especially in Bayesian inference — we only know $p$ up to a normalizing constant $p = \tilde{p} / Z$. While we’re interested in estimating $w = p / q$, we’re left holding the bag with it’s unnormalized cousin where $Z$ is unknown,

\[\begin{align*} w(x) = \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} = \frac{1}{Z}\frac{\tilde{p}(x)}{q(x)} = \frac{1}{Z} \tilde{w}(x) \end{align*}\]

which makes us arrive again at the same spot we started from that we don’t know $Z$ in the first place. But with unnormalized weights $\tilde{w}$, there is a trick we can pull to bootstrap ourselves out of this seemingly dead end.

The key observation is that the normalizing constant $Z$ is itself is an integral over the whole space $x$ of $\tilde{p}(x)$. We can therefore use importance sampling to evaluate the integral as in

\[Z = \int \tilde{p}(x)\,dx \int \frac{q(x)}{q(x)} \tilde{p}(x) = \int \ q(x) \ \frac{\tilde{p}(x)}{q(x)}\,dx = \int q(x) \ \tilde{w}(x) dx = \mathbb{E}_q[\bar{w}(X)]\]

Given that we’ve generated the samples $x \sim q$ proportionally to the distribution $q$ by definition, the expectation reduces to a simple average over the samples $x$,

\[\hat{Z} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \bar{w}(X_i)\]

Going back to our original objective we want to identify

\[\begin{align*} \hat{\mu}_{\text{IS}} &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w(X_i)\, f(X_i) \\ &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{p(X_i)}{q(X_i)}\, f(X_i) \\ &=\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{Z}\frac{\tilde{p}(X_i)}{q(X_i)}\, f(X_i) \\ &\approx \frac{1}{N} \frac{1}{\hat{Z}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}(X_i)\, f(X_i) \\ &= \frac{1}{N} \frac{1}{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}(X_i)} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}(X_i)\, f(X_i) \\ &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}(X_i)\, f(X_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}(X_i)} \\ \end{align*}\]

Whereas the importance estimator $\hat{\mu}{\text{IS}}$ assumes access to the target distribution $p$ including its normalization constant $Z$, using the unnormalized weights $\tilde{w}$ to estimate $\hat{Z}$ turns this estimator it to the _self-normalized estimator $\hat{\mu}_{\text{SN}}$.

We can simplify the equation for $\hat{\mu}_{\text{SN}}$ even further by observing that the sum in the denominator is self contained, so we can write \(\begin{align*} \hat{\mu}_{\text{SN}} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}(X_i)\, f(X_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \tilde{w}(X_j)} \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\tilde{w}(X_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \tilde{w}(X_j)}\, f(X_i) \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bar{w}(X_i)\, f(X_i) \\ \end{align*}\)

where the normalized weights $\bar{w}$ are defined as

\[\bar{w}_i = \frac{\tilde{w}_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_j}\]

Variance of the i.i.d. baseline

For comparison, if we had $N_{\text{eff}}$ i.i.d. samples from $p$, the variance of the sample mean would be:

\[\text{Var}\!\left[\frac{1}{N_{\text{eff}}}\sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{eff}}} f(X_i)\right] = \frac{\sigma_f^2}{N_{\text{eff}}}\]

where $\sigma_f^2 = \text{Var}_p[f(X)]$.

Variance of the self-normalized estimator

Now we need the variance of $\hat{\mu}_{\text{SN}}$. Using a delta-method / linearization argument (treating the ratio of two sample means), one can show that to first order:

\[\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_{\text{SN}}] \approx \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{\mathbb{E}_q[w^2(X)\,(f(X) - \mu)^2]}{\left(\mathbb{E}_q[w(X)]\right)^2}\]

For the purpose of defining ESS, we want a function-independent diagnostic (otherwise you’d need a different ESS for every $f$). So we focus on the dominant factor coming from the weight variability alone. The key insight is that the weight variance inflates the estimator variance by a factor related to the second moment of the weights.

Consider the simplified case $f(x) = 1$ (or more precisely, factor out $\sigma_f^2$). The variance inflation comes entirely from:

\[\text{Var}[\hat{\mu}_{\text{SN}}] \propto \frac{\sigma_f^2}{N} \cdot \frac{\mathbb{E}_q[w^2]}{(\mathbb{E}_q[w])^2}\]

Setting up the equivalence

We define $N_{\text{eff}}$ by equating:

\[\frac{\sigma_f^2}{N_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{\sigma_f^2}{N} \cdot \frac{\mathbb{E}_q[w^2]}{(\mathbb{E}_q[w])^2}\]

The $\sigma_f^2$ cancels (this is why ESS is function-independent), giving:

\[\boxed{N_{\text{eff}} = N \cdot \frac{(\mathbb{E}_q[w])^2}{\mathbb{E}_q[w^2]}}\]

The sample-level estimate

Replacing population moments with sample moments:

\[\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} = N \cdot \frac{\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum_i w_i\right)^2}{\frac{1}{N}\sum_i w_i^2} = \frac{\left(\sum_i w_i\right)^2}{\sum_i w_i^2}\]

Or equivalently, in terms of the normalized weights $\tilde{w}_i$:

\[\boxed{\hat{N}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{w}_i^2}}\]

This is the famous formula. Note it equals $N$ when all weights are equal ($\tilde{w}_i = 1/N$), and equals $1$ when a single weight dominates.

3.2 Connection to Rényi Divergence

The population-level ESS has an elegant information-theoretic interpretation. Recall that the Rényi divergence of order $\alpha = 2$ between $p$ and $q$ is:

\[D_2(p \| q) = \log \int \frac{p^2(x)}{q(x)}\, dx = \log\, \mathbb{E}_q[w^2(X)]\]

(using $\mathbb{E}_q[w] = 1$ when $p$ and $q$ are both normalized). Then:

\[N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{N}{\exp(D_2(p \| q))}\]

So ESS decays exponentially with the Rényi-2 divergence between target and proposal. This gives you a direct information-geometric lens: importance sampling “works” when $q$ is close to $p$ in the $D_2$ sense.

3.3 The MCMC / Autocorrelation Case

For MCMC, the samples $X_1, \dots, X_N$ are from the correct target $p$ (asymptotically), but they are correlated. Here the setup differs.

The variance of the MCMC sample mean is:

\[\text{Var}\!\left[\bar{f}_N\right] = \frac{\sigma_f^2}{N}\left(1 + 2\sum_{k=1}^{N-1}\left(1 - \frac{k}{N}\right)\rho_k\right)\]

where $\rho_k = \text{Corr}[f(X_t), f(X_{t+k})]$ is the lag-$k$ autocorrelation of the chain.

For large $N$, this simplifies to:

\[\text{Var}[\bar{f}_N] \approx \frac{\sigma_f^2}{N} \cdot \tau\]

where $\tau = 1 + 2\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \rho_k$ is the integrated autocorrelation time (IAT).

Setting this equal to the i.i.d. baseline $\sigma_f^2 / N_{\text{eff}}$:

\[\boxed{N_{\text{eff}} = \frac{N}{\tau} = \frac{N}{1 + 2\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \rho_k}}\]

Intuition: if consecutive samples have autocorrelation $\rho_1 \approx 0.95$, the chain is barely moving between steps. You might need ~40 samples to get the equivalent of one independent draw. The IAT captures exactly this — it’s the “number of steps per independent sample.”

4. Summary Table

Setting ESS Formula What drives it down
Importance sampling $\left(\sum w_i\right)^2 / \sum w_i^2$ Weight variance (proposal ≠ target)
MCMC $N / \tau$ Autocorrelation (slow mixing)
Particle filters Both effects compound Resampling + propagation

5. Practical Notes

  • ESS ≪ N is a red flag. For importance sampling, a common rule of thumb is to worry when $N_{\text{eff}} / N < 0.5$ or even $< 0.1$.
  • ESS is necessary but not sufficient. High ESS doesn’t guarantee your sampler has explored all modes — it only measures weight/correlation quality conditional on where you’ve sampled.
  • For MCMC, estimating $\tau$ reliably is itself nontrivial (see Geyer’s initial monotone sequence estimator, or the autowindow method in Stan).
  • The $1/\sum \tilde{w}_i^2$ formula is sometimes called the Kong (1992) or Liu (1996) effective sample size, after the authors who formalized it in the context of sequential Monte Carlo.
